Name: Kalutara Bodhiya
User_Review: I recently visited Kalutara Bodhiya and was truly moved by its serene and spiritual atmosphere. The temple is beautifully maintained, with the largest and one of the most architecturally unique Bodhi trees in Sri Lanka, surrounded by peaceful gardens and the soothing aroma of incense. Walking inside the hollow stupa and viewing the intricate paintings was a fascinating experience, and the underground tunnel connecting the two sides of the temple adds to its charm. It’s a must-visit for anyone seeking calm, cultural insight, and a deep connection to Buddhist heritage. Just a tip: try to avoid Sundays for a more peaceful visit. Highly recommended! 🙏

Name: Kalutara Bodhiya
User_Review:
Kalutara Bodhiya, located beside the Kalu River, is one of the 32 saplings from the original Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi, planted during the 2nd century BC. Despite colonial-era threats, the tree survived and remains a powerful symbol of Buddhist faith and national heritage.

Next to it stands the white Kalutara Chaitya—a rare hollow stupa housing 74 murals and small inner stupas depicting the Buddha's life. The entire site offers a peaceful, spiritual atmosphere, especially on full moon (Poya) days when devotees come in large numbers to worship.

Name: Kalutara Bodhiya
User_Review: Since childhood, I have visited the Kalutara Temple, and what I admire most is that it has remained the same over the years. The temple has preserved its humble peace and spiritual closeness, which makes every visit even more precious. Unlike many other temples that are crowded with new buildings and constructions, Kalutara Temple has been beautifully maintained, keeping its heritage look intact.

Name: Adams Peak Sri Pada Base
User_Review:
Sri Pada is a mountain in central Sri Lanka, about 2,243 meters (around 7,360 ft) high.
It is considered sacred by multiple religions. For Buddhists, there is a footprint-shaped rock near the summit believed to be that of the Buddha. Hindus say it’s of Shiva, some Christians and Muslims believe it’s the footprint of Adam or other figures.
Many pilgrims climb overnight so they can reach the summit in time to see the sunrise (“Ira Sevaya”).
Along the journey there are customary stops: places to rest, small shrines, places for offerings.
The trails go through beautiful landscapes: tropical forests, mist-covered hills, tea plantations, streams.
The surrounding area is part of the Peak Wilderness Sanctuary, which has rich biodiversity and is a protected natural area.
Watching the sunrise from the top is one of the most memorable parts. The view, the light, the feeling of being above the clouds — many say it’s unforgettable.
Also, at sunrise, due to the shape of the mountain, a triangular shadow is cast onto surrounding terrain — a visual phenomenon many visitors try to witness.

Name: Adams Peak Sri Pada Base
User_Review: Climbing Adam’s Peak during the day was a really nice activity. The hike itself was peaceful, and reaching the top felt amazing with the cool breeze and beautiful views all around. A memorable experience!

Name: Adams Peak Sri Pada Base
User_Review: Best is to start climbing at around 3am, the hike usually last less than 2 hours so you'll arrive at 5am just in time for sunrise.

Name: Adams Peak Sri Pada Base
User_Review:
Climbing Sri Pada, also known as Adam’s Peak, is not just a hike – it’s a spiritual and cultural journey that brings together people of all faiths and backgrounds. Rising over 2,200 meters, this sacred mountain is one of the most iconic landmarks in Sri Lanka, visited by thousands of pilgrims and travelers every year.

The hike usually begins at night, with pilgrims starting their climb around midnight to reach the summit before sunrise. The trail is lit with lanterns during the season, creating a magical atmosphere as you ascend step by step. The climb is challenging, with thousands of stone steps, but the energy of fellow travelers, chanting, and the dedication of pilgrims make the experience truly inspiring.

At the summit, you’ll find the sacred footprint (believed differently by Buddhists, Hindus, Christians, and Muslims) and breathtaking views stretching across the central highlands. The most unforgettable moment is the sunrise – watching the first rays of light spread across the mountains and the unique shadow of the peak forming a perfect triangular shape in the mist below.

The descent can be just as memorable, as daylight reveals tea plantations, mountain ranges, and the vibrant life of the villages around. It’s tough on the legs, but every step feels rewarding.

Whether you go for spiritual reasons or simply for the adventure, Sri Pada is an experience of devotion, endurance, and natural beauty that stays with you forever.

Name: Adams Peak Sri Pada Base
User_Review:
This is a must-visit place for every foreigner! You can truly feel the beauty and serenity of nature surrounding these mountains—it’s unbelievable. The morning sunrise is cinematic, and you won’t believe your eyes.
If you have good fitness, I highly recommend not climbing from the Hatton side. Instead, start from the Palabaddala or Kuruvita routes, as these are the most adventurous and rewarding paths.
Special note: All goods along the way rise in price the higher you go. It’s not a scam—these are normal local prices. I recommend bringing all essentials with you, including boots, jackets, ice caps, and gloves. Also, make sure your backpack is light, not heavy, to make the climb easier.

Name: Ratnapura Gem Museum
User_Review:
The Rathnapura National Museum once the official residence of Ahelepola Adikaram, is now converted into a historic landmark in Rathnapura. The interior comprises of 09 sections to walk you through the pre-historic significance of the area, abundant minerals and rocks, cultural significance and most importantly the belongings of Ahelepola Adikaram, who once was a dominant character in the latter stage of Royal dynasties in Kandyan Kingdom.

The entrance ticket is 100/- (local adult) and 50/- (local child) and the place is well maintained. The building is surrounded by a herbal garden with botanical importance and a courtyard of surrounding buildings.

A must visit location for explorers of the history of Rathnapura.

Name: Ratnapura Gem Museum
User_Review: The Ratnapura Museum is a must-visit destination that beautifully completes your journey through the city. After exploring other places in Ratnapura, coming here helps you truly understand their real historical and cultural value. The museum not only showcases Ratnapura’s heritage but also takes you back to the prehistoric era. Housed in the ancient administrative building of Ahalepola Maha Adhikaram, it’s surrounded by a peaceful garden where you can relax and reflect on what you’ve learned.

Name: Ratnapura Gem Museum
User_Review:
The Ratnapura National Museum, housed in the historic Ehelepola Walawwa, offers a captivating glimpse into the rich cultural and historical heritage of the Sabaragamuwa Province. Once the residence of Ehelepola Nilame, a regional officer under King Sri Vikrama Rajasinghe, this site now beautifully preserves the essence of Sri Lanka's past. The exhibits range from prehistoric artifacts to more recent historical items, including a fascinating display of ancient gem mining techniques for which Ratnapura is world-renowned. The museum also features replicas of endemic animals and a lush, well-maintained garden that adds to the experience. As a history enthusiast, I found the blend of regional stories and national significance deeply enriching. A must-visit if you're exploring Sri Lanka’s interior.
Tickets are very cheap and it's open Saturday.

Name: Bopath Ella Waterfall
User_Review:
The google map around here has not updated for years. The waterfall is accessible for the nearest place with any passenger vehicle.

Bopath Falls in Ratnapura is a truly beautiful waterfall, surrounded by lush greenery and a serene natural setting. The waterfall’s unique Bo‑leaf shape adds a special charm, making it one of the most picturesque spots in Sri Lanka. The entrance cost is very low, making it accessible for anyone looking for a simple yet memorable nature experience.

From the entrance, there is a short and easy walk to the main viewing area. The waterfall looks absolutely stunning from this safe zone, with the sound of rushing water adding to the calm and refreshing atmosphere. It’s a great place to take photographs, enjoy a peaceful moment, or spend time with friends and family.

However, it is extremely dangerous to climb the waterfall. According to the guards at the site, around 88 deaths had been reported up to the date of our visit, mostly caused by people attempting to climb or get too close to the slippery rocks. For safety reasons, it is strongly recommended to enjoy the waterfall only from designated safe areas.

Parking is available nearby, with a small charge for the larger lot about 200–300 meters from the entrance. There is also a smaller parking space closer to the gate for convenience.

Overall, Bopath Falls is a must-visit attraction in Sri Lanka. It offers stunning natural beauty at a very low cost and is perfect for families, friends, or solo travelers. While safety is essential, the experience is truly unforgettable and highly recommended for anyone visiting Ratnapura.

Name: Bopath Ella Waterfall
User_Review:
Bopath Ella is a 30-metre waterfall in the Ratnapura District, Sri Lanka, named for its resemblance to the leaf of the sacred Bo tree.

There is a designated car park operated by the local council (Kuruwita Pradeshiya Sabha). Vehicles must be parked there, and from the parking area you walk to the viewpoint.

A small ticket fee is required to access the waterfall area (Locals Rs. 50 and Tourists Rs. 300).

A toilet complex is constructed near the waterfall by the local council. There are also private restrooms (some attached to places with lodging).

Along the footpath from the parking/viewpoint to the waterfall there are small shops/vendors offering snacks, drinks, and local food. Also there are guest-houses/restaurants nearby with more substantial meals.

There is a viewing area (platform or stairs) near the falls. You have to walk from the car park through a shaded, scenic path. Rocks may be slippery; caution advised.

A very beautiful waterfall located in the Ratnapura district.A very easily accessible waterfall.You must purchase a ticket to visit this waterfall.The waterfall is about 30m high.A place that has attracted many tourists.Parking and toilet facilities are available.There are places where you can swim safely.Don't pollute the environment.

Our instructors were incredibly friendly and professional, ensuring that we felt safe at all times while also encouraging us to fully enjoy the adventure. The breathtaking scenery of Kithulgala truly made the experience special. Rafting down the rapids was exhilarating, and we created incredible memories together that will last a lifetime.

I highly recommend this experience to anyone looking for a fun and adventurous day out. Whether you're a seasoned rafter or a beginner, the staff here will make sure you have a fantastic time.

Thank you to the wonderful instructors for making our day so memorable! We can't wait to return for another adventure!

Name: Kitulgala Adventure Camp
User_Review:
Would repeat the experience I had with this place! There are many rafting experiences to choose from and my husband and I ended up finding this one on Google Maps. We were happy with our choice! They even offered to pick us up from a nearby bus station? They made sure all the gear fit, and gave us a full safety training.

So the communication was the best part of it all. We ended up choosing the second package, a full experience for 30 USD per person for 3.5 hours. We experienced canyoning, zip-lining, and rafting and were even able to go multiple times!

Would come back :)

Name: Kitulgala Adventure Camp
User_Review: Very good experience, highly recommend!! :) thank you!

Name: Kitulgala Adventure Camp
User_Review:
Had a great day rafting and canyoning with the guys. Communication was pretty fast and easy via What's App. They could provide more details about the equipment you get. Basically just a swimming vest ist provided. For girls it's good to know that you should probably wear long trousers and a t-shirt as it's part of the culture to cover up and also helpful for the Canyoning as you're sliding down rocks and jumping in the water.
The changing room and shower are pretty basic but the equipment is okay and the guides do their best to explain things and answer questions. So if you wanna have a fun day with new experiences go for it and enjoy the beautiful landscape and easy rafting and canyoning. Slides are slow, jumps medium and the rafting quite relaxed. Just ask all the questions upfront as they won't give u much information without asking. Best of all: we were just two people, so a really small group and asked to go straightaway in the morning as then you're basically alone as all the other groups start around lunch.

Name: Kitulgala Adventure Camp
User_Review: Had a mind blowing experience with the team Adventure Lk. Mr Hasindu was super helpful from the day I contacted him to the last minute. He helped us with the accommodation and even finding the bus route. They definitely deserve all 5 starts for their efforts. Great job keep it up guys

Name: Elephant Freedom Project
User_Review: What an amazing morning spent with these two lovely ladies! Manike and Kumari are the sweetest elephants and they are clearly loved and well cared for. The staff is wonderful and welcoming. This is the only ethical (read no riding!) sanctuary in Sri Lanka. It's a wonderful way to spend time with elephants in their natural habitat. They have to freedom to move about as they please and seem to genuinely like interacting with people. I can't say enough good things.  If you want to see elephants in Sri Lanka, please come here and not the riding places.

Name: Elephant Freedom Project
User_Review: Wow! What an incredible day! Such an intimate experience with the two beautiful elephants! So lovely to see the elephants being treated so well too! This definitely needs to be on your Sri Lankan itinerary! I will never forget this. Thank you so much for having us!

Name: Elephant Freedom Project
User_Review: This was an amazing experience for our family.  The elephants are so well cared for and treated ethically, no chains!  The elephants are able to live free as they should.  The people there are fantastic, so caring, compassionate and knowledgeable.  This is a must experience if you visit Sri Lanka.  And make sure to stay for lunch which is homemade and so good!  We truly appreciated this experience and the care of these majestic creatures.

Name: Elephant Freedom Project
User_Review:
Wonderful morning with the 2 elephants at the project, following them around their land and washing them. You can tell the staff all really care for the elephants and believe in what they are doing. Hopefully more projects like this can save all of the captive elephants in Sri Lanka!

All staff were extremely friendly and knowledgeable. It was extremely hot so bring sun cream, bug repellent and water shoes!

Lunch after was also a delicious, homemade non spicy meal!

Car park is opposite and free :)

Name: Elephant Freedom Project
User_Review:
Why you should visit this elephant orphanage.

1. It’s the only ethical orphanage in Sri Lanka.
2. The staff have respect for the elephants.
3. The elephants have their freedom as you can witnesses them eating, bathing and taking their time.
4. They love bathing and will relax while you scrub them and shower them with water and much love.
5. They do not hurt these elephants or poke them with any sharp objects.
6. You’ll get a yummy home made lunch.
7. You’ll go home feeling happy that you have contributed to their care.
8. Looking back on you hugging and giving kisses to the elephants while they’re relaxed in the water.

Name: Kithulgala River Bridge
User_Review: Highly recommended! I had a great time. I was a solo joiner and was grouped with a family from belgium. Our guide Pawan is so professional. He also made me do all the jumps and slides during the canyoneering, which i enjoyed so much. Also the bigboss Ruwan was kind enough to answer all my questions and help me coordinate the whole trip. I cant thank him enough. Everyone who travel to Sri Lanka really needs to visit Kithulgala!

Name: Kithulgala River Bridge
User_Review:
We had an amazing experience with them! We did canyoning with our girls ages 12,10,7, which was really extreme, a very unique experience, we enjoyed every moment! The next day we did rafting, which was also a great experience. Their guide was professional and really cute.
Really recommend this company!

Name: Kithulgala River Bridge
User_Review:
Me and a group of 14 friends went water rafting with Sri Lanka Green Kitulgala Water Rafting Adventure on 12th April 2025, and the experience was absolutely amazing! I had already pre-booked the session, and everything was perfectly arranged and ready when we arrived.

This was my second time going water rafting, but my first time with this team, and it still felt so new and exciting! A big thank you to Ruwan and the entire crew for making it such a smooth and fun adventure.

Name: Kithulgala River Bridge
User_Review: Great experience and adventure with an awesome crew. Ruwan and his team did their best to make this trip amazing. Well organized, you feel yourself always safe and as a valued customer. Starting with the whatsapp message to book till the lunch and saying goodbye. Everything was perfect for us! We were 9 with 2 kids (6 and 7) but they also enjoyed everything. Rafting, transport, canyoning, sliding... I can only recommend Ruwan and his team, and I would say this trip is a must have if you are in Sri Lanka.

Name: Kithulgala River Bridge
User_Review:
Absolute epic experience! Ruwan (owner) and Chaminda (our guide) went out of their way to make our group comfortable and we had a great day.
The river and the surrounding forest is so gorgeous ❤️

Name: Saman Devale Temple
User_Review: A sacred and historic temple dedicated to God Saman, the guardian deity of Sri Pada (Adam’s Peak). The temple has a calm and spiritual atmosphere, attracting both devotees and visitors. Its traditional architecture, cultural significance, and annual Perahera (procession) make it an important religious landmark in Ratnapura. A peaceful place to experience Sri Lanka’s heritage and devotion.

Name: Saman Devale Temple
User_Review:
Sri Sumana Saman Devalaya is a famous religious place in Ratnapura, Sri Lanka. It is dedicated to God Saman, one of the guardian deities of the island. The devalaya is located near the Kalu Ganga and is visited by many pilgrims every year, especially during the Sri Pada pilgrimage season.

The temple has a peaceful environment and beautiful ancient architecture. People come here to worship and pray for blessings and protection. It is also an important cultural site with historical value. The annual Esala festival held at the devalaya is colorful and full of traditional dances and rituals.

This devalaya shows the deep respect Sri Lankans have for their spiritual traditions and local gods.

Name: Saman Devale Temple
User_Review: 🕊️ Sri Sumana Saman Devalaya is a serene and spiritual place, ideal for anyone seeking peace and blessings. Devotees can bring fruit offerings (fruit watti) to worship God Sumana Saman, but please note that king coconut is not allowed, and fruits should not be pre-cut. The temple staff will prepare your offerings with their own plates.  hours are from 7:00 AM to 12:00 noon and again from 6:00 PM to 9:30 PM. A truly peaceful and sacred experience. 🌿🙏

Name: Saman Devale Temple
User_Review:
Maha Saman Devalaya, also known as Sumana Saman Devalaya, is a shrine in Ratnapura, Sri Lanka, dedicated to the deity Saman. He is the guardian of Sri Pada (also called Samanthakuta), a sacred mountain believed to bear the Buddha’s left footprint from his visit to Sri Lanka.

When you enter the Dewalaya, you can feel a deep sense of calm. The surroundings are quiet. It is a great spot to relax your mind and feel close to nature.

The architecture of the temple is simple yet elegant. During festival times, especially the Perahera, the Saman Devalaya becomes full of life with colorful processions and traditional dances.

Whether you go there for religious reasons or just to enjoy the peaceful environment, Saman Dewalaya is a wonderful place to visit. It gives both spiritual comfort and natural beauty in one place.

Name: Saman Devale Temple
User_Review:
The Maha Saman Devalaya, located in Ratnapura, Sri Lanka, is a revered shrine dedicated to deity Saman, considered one of the island's guardian deities and the protector of Sri Pada (Adam's Peak). The temple's origins are traced back to 1270, when it was founded by Aryakamadeva during the Dambadeniya period. ​

Throughout its history, the shrine has experienced periods of destruction and restoration. Notably, between 1618 and 1620, the Portuguese built a fort and church on the temple grounds. Later, King Keerthi Sri Rajasinghe (1747–1781) of the Kandyan Kingdom recaptured Ratnapura, demolished these structures, and reconstructed the Maha Saman Devalaya, which is believed to be the structure that stands today. ​

The temple complex features two platforms: the lower platform accessible through two vahalkadas (entrance structures) on the east and south, and the upper platform reached via a flight of steps on the eastern side. ​

Annually, the Maha Saman Devalaya hosts the Saman Devala Perahera, a vibrant procession that showcases traditional Sri Lankan culture and attracts numerous devotees and tourists. ​

Name: Aberdeen Falls Entry Point
User_Review:
Aberdeen Falls is one of the most beautiful waterfalls in Sri Lanka, named after a city in Scotland. Its beauty is truly beyond words, absolutely stunning and worth the visit. No need of buying ticket here and its was so peaceful when visiting on a weekday.

As far as I know, there are two access routes to the falls, though I’ve only taken one of them. I’ve heard that the other route leads to the top of the waterfall, but I doubt it offers a full view of the falls.
The route I took was from the Aberdeen Feel Resort side. There’s parking available, and from there it’s about a 1 km walk to reach the waterfall. The main advantage of this route is that you can reach the bottom of the waterfall, whereas the other route only allows you to view it from a bridge. So it’s worth deciding which route you prefer depending on what experience you want.

There are several warning signs indicating that bathing is dangerous, so it’s best to simply enjoy the breathtaking view and return safely.

Name: Aberdeen Falls Entry Point
User_Review: One of the most beautiful waterfalls in Sri Lanka. Stunning waterfall hidden in the lush green hills of Ginigathhena. The hike down takes about 30–40 minutes through the forest, but it’s worth it — the view of Aberdeen Falls surrounded by rocks and greenery is breathtaking. Perfect for photography. A peaceful, less crowded place to enjoy nature.Visit early morning for the best light and fewer people.

Name: Aberdeen Falls Entry Point
User_Review:
Aberdeen Waterfall is one of the most stunning waterfalls I’ve visited in Sri Lanka. The walk to the falls is about 1.2 km through a peaceful forest, and although there are many steps and the return climb can be a little tiring, the view at the end is absolutely worth it.

The waterfall is tall, powerful, and breathtaking, with a natural pool at the base that looks inviting, but be careful as it can be deep and dangerous during the rainy season. The viewing platform gives a perfect spot for photography and enjoying the scenery.

Best time to visit is after the rains when the water flow is strong and the surroundings are lush green. Wear good shoes, carry water, and take your time on the path. Please remember to keep the place clean and carry your trash back.

A truly refreshing experience — highly recommended for nature lovers and anyone looking for a little adventure!

Name: Aberdeen Falls Entry Point
User_Review: Aberdeen Waterfall is beautiful. The calm atmosphere, surrounded by lush greenery and the gentle sound of flowing water, offers a perfect getaway from the city. The short walk to the falls is picturesque and easy, so most people can enjoy it. It's a great place for photos, a picnic, or simply to relax and appreciate nature. It's definitely worth visiting if you're nearby.

Name: Aberdeen Falls Entry Point
User_Review: Another beautiful waterfall in Sri Lanka is Aberdeen Falls. To access the falls, you have to descend about 600 steps.

Name: Anuradhapura Sacred City
User_Review:
Clean rooms, a super comfortable bed, and a beautiful outdoor area – almost like having your own little jungle. The owners are incredibly warm, kind, and helpful, and they are taking the time for personal conversations ☺️

Breakfast and dinner were fantastic – always something new and delicious, and even specially prepared vegetarian meals for us.

On our very first evening, we were invited to join a Buddhist ceremony in the village – an unforgettable experience! The tuk-tuk tour through the temple complex with the hotel’s own guide was also amazing. He knows so much and explains everything in great detail.

Anuradhapura is already a beautiful and culturally rich place, but this stay made our time there absolutely perfect. The owners run their hotel with so much heart that we honestly didn’t want to leave – and in the end, we stayed an extra night!

Name: Anuradhapura Sacred City
User_Review: The hosts are genuinely lovely and honest with excellent English. Really good Sri Lankan breakfasts made completely from scratch each day, with good air and very clean basic rooms with fresh and well laundered linen. Good location within walking distance of some of the key sites, but there are a few flights of stairs to be navigated, which might not suit anyone with mobility issues. I gave a wee tip as thanks when I settled the bill and shortly after received a knock on my door because they thought I had overpaid. I can't remember that happening before on my travels!

Name: Anuradhapura Sacred City
User_Review:
Fantastic hotel in the city centre. Rooms are clean and beds have mosquito nets. The hotel has a fantastic rooftop with alle kind of plants, flowers, spices.
The only minor is that sometimes the waterpressure is low to nothing. Just a signal to the owner and the pressure is back again. Ohw...I showered with cold water. No idea how to turn on hot water but cold was a gift due to the heat. But of you really want to shower with hot water: ask the owner. The hotel offers different Stuppa and ruin tours and cloth can be washed.

Name: Anuradhapura Sacred City
User_Review: Wonderful stay here! Nice rooms & kind owners! Thank you 🙏

Name: Anuradhapura Sacred City
User_Review: Lovely accommodation central to the town. Definitely a hidden gem and surprisingly quiet considering how close to the road it is. Staff are so friendly and were accommodating for my vegan diet. Beautiful roof top views of the surrounding areas and a joyful little dog called Murphy who runs around the place. Wonderful place, wonderful people!

Name: Ruwanwelisaya Stupa
User_Review:
Ruwanwelisaya is a truly magnificent and spiritually uplifting place to visit. This ancient stupa, built by King Dutugemunu, stands as a symbol of devotion, peace, and Buddhist heritage. Its gleaming white dome and grand architecture are breathtaking, especially when illuminated at night. The surroundings are calm and well-maintained, creating a serene atmosphere for meditation and reflection. Pilgrims from all over the world visit to pay their respects and offer flowers. Visiting Ruwanwelisaya is an unforgettable experience that fills your heart with peace and admiration for Sri Lanka’s ancient civilization.
☸️🤍

Name: Ruwanwelisaya Stupa
User_Review: රුවන්වැලි සෑය truly feels like a sacred and peaceful place. The stupa is breathtaking in its size and beauty, especially in the evening when it’s lit up. The surroundings are very clean and well maintained, and the atmosphere is calm and spiritual. It’s a must-visit location in Anuradhapura for anyone interested in history, culture, or Buddhism. A place that fills your heart with serenity and respect.

Name: Ruwanwelisaya Stupa
User_Review:
One of the most sacred places if not the most, for Sri Lankan Buddhists. You can truly feel the wonder of this monument as the entire surrounding area seems illuminated by its pure whiteness. The gigantic size of this great stupa reminds you how small human life is compared to spiritual greatness, as seen in many ancient religious structures.

The Great Stupa (Ruwanwelisaya) is said to have been built by King Dutugemunu around 140 BC. It has now been fully restored, with all its architectural components in place, allowing pilgrims to worship freely.

Being both spiritually and historically significant, this site is also admired for its remarkable ancient engineering and project management.

The place is usually packed with pilgrims on weekends, holidays, and Poya days, from early morning until late at night. However, visiting on such days offers a chance to witness various rituals (poojas) performed by local devotees. Foreign visitors interested in Sinhala Buddhist traditions and culture will find these experiences especially meaningful.

Name: Ruwanwelisaya Stupa
User_Review:
The most sacred place in Sri Lanka which was built in early 140 BC by King Dutugemunu in significance to ending the war and uniting the country. After restoration it’s now almost 330 feet higher in shape of a Bubble. It houses sacred relics (a Drona) of Lord Buddha which makes this place the nost sacred place in Sri Lanka
Foreigners, locals, monks, pilgrims are always doing their religious rituals in this place so silence is the most required discipline expected here
There are separate times for the “Theva” to be praised towards the Maha stupa and Lord Buddha. This is also a UNESCO world heritage site in Anuradapura which is included in the Solosmastana and Atamasthana also.
Please note that you may nostalgic probably have to be in long queues on holidays and weekends.

Name: Ruwanwelisaya Stupa
User_Review:
Truly a majestic and spiritual place!
Ruwanweli Maha Seya is one of the most breathtaking sights in Sri Lanka. The stupa is massive (Largest in Sri Lanka) and beautifully maintained, especially when it’s lit up at night — absolutely stunning! The atmosphere is so peaceful, filled with the sound of prayers and the smell of incense. You can really feel the sacred energy here. It’s a must-visit spot in Anuradhapura, whether you’re here for the history, architecture, or spiritual experience. We visited Ruwanweli Maha Seya both daytime and night.I could have stayed for hours just taking it all in. 🙏✨

Name: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi Tree
User_Review:
Sri Maha Bodhi is one of the most sacred and spiritually significant places in Sri Lanka. It is said to be the oldest living tree in the world with a recorded history, brought from India over 2,000 years ago by Sangamitta Theri. For Buddhists, it is a deeply revered site, as this is a branch of the Bodhi tree under which Lord Buddha attained Enlightenment.

The atmosphere here is truly special  calm, spiritual, and filled with devotion. Pilgrims from all over the country come to offer flowers, light lamps, and pray. The temple grounds are well-maintained, and the evening ceremonies with chanting and lights create a deeply moving experience.

Visitors should wear white or modest clothing and be respectful, as this is a living place of worship. It’s best to visit in the morning or evening when the weather is cooler.

Name: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi Tree
User_Review:
The Sri Maha Bodhi is one of the most sacred and peaceful places I have ever visited. This ancient sacred fig tree, believed to have grown from a branch of the original Bodhi tree under which Lord Buddha attained enlightenment, carries immense spiritual value. The atmosphere is calm, filled with prayers, devotion, and the sound of temple bells. The area is beautifully maintained, and the monks and visitors show great respect and reverence. Visiting the Sri Maha Bodhi is a deeply meaningful experience that connects you to Sri Lanka’s rich Buddhist heritage and timeless serenity.
☸️🤍

Name: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi Tree
User_Review:
One of the most sacred places for Buddhists. The ancient Bo tree inside the golden fence is said to be a branch of the original 'Asathu' Bo tree which was utilised by the lord Buddha on his enlightenment. The place has a huge significance with regard to Sri Lankan history as well.
Very crowded on week ends, holidays and poya days. If you are not a VIP you have to walk about a kilo metre to enter the sacred premises. A must see for foreign tourists interested in Sri Lankan culture and traditions.

Name: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi Tree
User_Review:
In the heart of Anuradhapura, surrounded by peace and devotion, stands one of the most sacred living symbols of Buddhism — the Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi. This sacred fig tree is believed to have grown from a branch of the original Bodhi Tree in Bodh Gaya, India, under which Lord Buddha attained Enlightenment more than 2,500 years ago.

The branch was brought to Sri Lanka in the 3rd century BC by Sangamitta Theri, the daughter of Emperor Ashoka of India, during the reign of King Devanampiyatissa. It was planted with great reverence in the royal gardens of Anuradhapura, and since then, it has stood as a living link to the Enlightenment of the Buddha.

For over two millennia, devotees from all corners of the world have visited the Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi to offer flowers, light oil lamps, and pray for blessings. It is said to be the oldest historically documented tree in the world, cared for and worshipped continuously throughout history.

Name: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi Tree
User_Review:
Under this tree  which Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) attained enlightenment. In 236 BC, the Buddhist nun Sangamitta Maha Theri, a daughter of Indian great emperor Ashoka. brought the tree to Sri Lanka during the reign of Sinhalese great King Devanampiya Tissa. At more than 2,300 years old, it is the oldest living human-planted tree in the world with a known planting date.

Today, the Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi is situated on a high terrace, about 6.5 meters above the ground, and surrounded by 4 other lower-level terraces with Bo trees called "Parivara Bodhi" planted for its protection.

Triple gems bless you!!!.

Namo Buddhaya!!!.

Name: Mihintale Ancient Monastery
User_Review: Mihintale Rajamaha Viharaya is a sacred and historic place, believed to be where Buddhism was first introduced to Sri Lanka. The climb with its ancient stone steps is memorable, and at the top you’re rewarded with stunning views and a calm, spiritual atmosphere. The stupas, caves, and statues add to the sense of history and devotion. A must-visit for both pilgrims and travelers

Name: Mihintale Ancient Monastery
User_Review:
One of the most important places in Sri Lankan history. This is where Arahant Mahinda, on his Dhamma mission, first met King Devanampiyatissa. Sri Lankan culture flourished after the introduction of Buddhism to its social fabric by Arahant Mahinda.

Several parts of the temple complex are built on rock surfaces, which can get extremely hot during the daytime. It’s almost impossible to walk on the sand or rocks barefoot, so it’s best to wear socks (as shoes must be removed before entering the temple premises). The Aradhana Gala is the rock top where Arahath Mahinda stood when he first called King Devanampiyatissa from his first name.

The path to the temple is made up of stone steps lined with Frangipani trees on both sides — a beautiful sight when viewed from the foot of the steps. There are two access points to the temple, each with its own car park. Visitors can either park at the foot of the mountain and climb the full distance, or drive partway up and continue on foot from there.

Name: Mihintale Ancient Monastery
User_Review: This is a sacred place located in a beautiful and fascinating area. To reach it, visitors must climb the mountain on foot, which adds a sense of spiritual journey and adventure. It is recommended to visit in the evening, as the daytime sun can be quite strong. The peaceful atmosphere and natural surroundings make it a truly rewarding experience.

Name: Mihintale Ancient Monastery
User_Review:
Mihinthalaya is a truly important place for Sri Lankan Buddhists, known as the begaining point of Buddha Dharmaya in Sri Lanka. The moment you enter, you feel the religional value and the peaceful, beautiful environment surrounding you. I parked our vehicle in the parking area and reached the temple through the beautiful rock staircase, which itself felt like a spiritual journey. But be careful, because some monkeys tried to get my juice bottle on the way!

There is a special place where you can safely leave your shoes before entering the temple, please make sure to leave your shoes there out of respect. I then visited Sela Chaithya, offered my worship, and continued climbing to the top of the Mihinthalaya rock. Standing there is unforgettable. It is the very starting place of Sri Lankan Buddhism. From the top you can see breathtaking views, few Chaithyas, and a serene Buddha statue around you. I reached in the evening, and the golden sunset sence across the horizon was simply magical.

Afterward, I worshiped Mihidu Maha Seya and Mininthala Maha Stupa, which filled me with kindness and calmness. I truly recommend everyone to visit this sacred place, not only as a tourist attraction but as a spiritual journey that connects your heart with the beginnings of Buddhism in Sri Lanka. A must-visit for anyone who loves history, culture, and peace. 🌅🙏✨

Name: Mihintale Ancient Monastery
User_Review:
My Visit to Mihintale Rock

Climbing Mihintale Rock was more than just a hike—it was a spiritual journey through history. As the sun rose over the ancient stone steps, each one seemed to whisper stories of centuries past. Known as the cradle of Buddhism in Sri Lanka, Mihintale felt sacred and timeless.

The path led me past ancient ruins, stone inscriptions, and quiet shrines. Monkeys darted through the trees, and the breeze carried the scent of wildflowers and incense. After the final climb, I stood at the summit, breathless—not just from the climb, but from the view. Rolling hills, distant stupas, and endless skies stretched before me

Name: Twin Baths Kuttam Pokuna
User_Review:
Kuttam Pokuna is one of the finest examples of ancient Sri Lankan hydraulic engineering. The twin ponds are beautifully preserved and showcase the brilliance of the Anuradhapura civilization. A must-see for anyone interested in history and architecture

Really enjoyed visiting Kuttam Pokuna! The ponds are so elegant, and the stone work is amazing. It’s a quiet spot that feels both historic and peaceful at the same tim

Name: Twin Baths Kuttam Pokuna
User_Review:
Kuttam Pokuna, also known as the Twin Ponds, is one of the most fascinating examples of ancient Sri Lankan engineering. Built by the monks of Anuradhapura, these twin bathing tanks are beautifully designed with granite steps leading down into the water. The symmetry and craftsmanship are truly remarkable, especially considering they were built more than a thousand years ago.

The site is calm and peaceful, surrounded by greenery, making it a perfect spot to relax while appreciating the brilliance of ancient hydraulic technology. It’s well-maintained, easy to access, and usually not overcrowded, so you can take your time to enjoy the view.

A must-visit when exploring the sacred city of Anuradhapura both for its historical value and for the quiet beauty it offers.

Name: Twin Baths Kuttam Pokuna
User_Review: These large twin ponds or pools are well preserved old bathing ponds in Sri Lanka. They were built by the Sinhalese in the ancient Anuradhapura Kingdom. They are important developments in the field of hydrological engineering, architecture and art of the ancient Sinhalese. Interesting to see them. Easy access and not much walking. Wear comfy shoes, hat, sunscreen, take water. Entry fee for the whole complex

Name: Twin Baths Kuttam Pokuna
User_Review:
Kuttam Pokuna is a truly wonderful ancient site, famous for its beautifully built ponds that were once used for bathing. Within the premises, there are a couple of ponds, all crafted entirely from stone. Standing there, you can clearly see the incredible technology and engineering skills that Sri Lanka had in ancient times. It is one of the best evidences of our proud history.

Parking facilities are available, and just nearby you will also find small shops where you can buy juices, snacks, and even gift items to take with you.

I warmly invite you to visit this incredible place to explore the ancient technology, history, and beauty that Kuttam Pokuna represents. It is not just a historical landmark, but also a reminder of how advanced and artistic our ancestors were. A must-see if you are visiting Anuradhapura. 🙏✨

Name: Twin Baths Kuttam Pokuna
User_Review:
Kuttam Pokuna, also known as the Twin Ponds, is an ancient bathing complex located in Anuradhapura, Sri Lanka. Built during the reign of the ancient Sinhalese kings, it is considered a masterpiece of hydrological engineering and architecture.

The two ponds are made from granite and are beautifully crafted with symmetrical design. Water was filtered and supplied through an underground system, showcasing the advanced technology of that era. These ponds were believed to have been used by Buddhist monks of the Abhayagiri Monastery for ritual bathing.

Kuttam Pokuna is famous for its aesthetic design, stone carvings, and the peaceful atmosphere surrounding it. The best time to visit is during the dry season, between May and September, when the weather is pleasant.

It is not only a historical site but also a symbol of the ingenuity and sophistication of ancient Sri Lankan civilization.

Name: Gal Vihara Rock Temple
User_Review:
Carved into the heart of an ancient granite rock in Polonnaruwa lies Gal Viharaya, a masterpiece of Sri Lanka’s medieval artistry and devotion. Known in its time as Uttararama, “the northern monastery,” this sacred site was created during the reign of King Parakramabahu I in the 12th century. It wasn’t just a temple—it was a spiritual and intellectual hub where monks gathered, meditated, and reformed the Buddhist monastic order. The king himself convened an assembly of monks here, establishing a code of conduct for the Sangha, inscriptions of which can still be found near the rock carvings.

The temple’s allure lies in its four colossal Buddha statues, all carved from a single stretch of granite but each telling a different story of enlightenment and serenity. The seated Buddha, over fifteen feet tall, rests in deep meditation upon a lotus throne adorned with lions and mythical makara motifs—symbols of strength and protection. Nearby, within an artificial cavern known as Vidyadhara Guha, another smaller seated figure glows with quiet grace, framed by a halo and faint traces of ancient frescoes. The standing figure, almost twenty-three feet tall, gazes downward with folded arms and an expression of sorrow. Scholars still debate whether this is the Buddha himself in contemplation or his disciple Ananda, mourning the Master’s passing. The journey culminates in the breathtaking reclining Buddha, stretching forty-six feet across the rock, portraying the Buddha entering Parinirvana—the final release from the cycle of rebirth—marked by serene features and delicate details like the single lotus etched on his palm and feet.

Every curve and contour of Gal Viharaya reflects architectural genius and spiritual purpose. The sculptors worked with the natural rise and fall of the rock, shaping each statue in harmony with its form. Even today, the site exudes an aura of calm reverence, its stone figures protected beneath a modern metal canopy that shields them from the elements. Visitors arriving at this sacred monument in Polonnaruwa, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, are reminded to dress modestly, remove their footwear, and step softly among the ancient echoes of faith. Standing before these timeless figures, one can almost feel the whispers of monks long past and the steady rhythm of a civilization that carved its devotion into stone for eternity.

Name: Gal Vihara Rock Temple
User_Review: Visiting Gal Viharaya was a truly special experience. The massive rock-cut Buddha statues are beautifully carved and full of expression, showing the incredible craftsmanship of ancient times. The site is peaceful and well cared for, creating a calm and spiritual atmosphere. I felt happy and honored to witness such an important part of Sri Lanka’s heritage. A must-visit when in Polonnaruwa.

Name: Gal Vihara Rock Temple
User_Review:
Visiting Polonnaruwa was like stepping into a timeless story written in stone. As one of Sri Lanka’s ancient capitals, this UNESCO World Heritage Site is a treasure trove of history, culture, and breathtaking architecture.

Walking through the well-preserved ruins — from majestic palaces and towering stupas to intricately carved statues like the famous Gal Vihara — we felt deeply connected to the rich past of this remarkable kingdom. The peaceful surroundings, combined with the grandeur of ancient Sri Lankan craftsmanship, created a truly unforgettable experience.

It’s a place that not only fascinates history lovers but also offers a serene, spiritual atmosphere for all who visit. Whether you're exploring by foot or bicycle, every corner of Polonnaruwa tells a story.

A must-visit destination for anyone traveling through Sri Lanka — full of wonder, wisdom, and timeless beauty.

Name: Gal Vihara Rock Temple
User_Review:
This particular site is 900 years old and was actually a devoted temple or worshipping place inside a cave. The cave was made from really thick walls and has been excavated so that the wall with the statues of Buddha can be easily seen by all.

One must definitely visit this place of not the others in the ancient kingdom city of Polunawara. It's simple yet awestruck kind of grandeur really reaches your soul.

There are four statues which from left to right stands for enlightenment, heaven, before death and death of Lord Buddha.

To imagine that this huge thing was done by hand centuries ago is simply mind boggling. We reached this site at night and it was still very visible thanks to the food lighting. Many dogs were sleeping around. I felt only positive vibes from visiting this place.

I would've loved to just sit there for sometime and just soak in the atmosphere. Seeing the dogs peacefully sleeping, I felt only one thing that if your mind and body are at peace, the place just doesn't matter. It's possibly what Lord Buddha attained in his lifetime and even though it's difficult to leave ones family the way he did...I do commend and appreciate his life endeavour. It has brought so much peace to others. His learnings are still followed. His presence even in the form of stone brings peace. I don't need to believe in anything but my visit here forced me to believe in the power of human mind and soul.

Name: Gal Vihara Rock Temple
User_Review: A peaceful and impressive place. The rock carved Buddha statues at Gal Viharaya are absolutely stunning and full of history. You can really feel the calm and sacred energy there. Definitely a must visit if you're exploring Polonnaruwa.

Name: Polonnaruwa Vatadage
User_Review:
This is one of the structures that are still kept erect in Pollonuwara. Beautiful artistic work or masonry.

People were so skilled back then and the skills were being valued unlike now. I would recommend a good two hours for seeing all important locations with a guide who can explain everything!

This is a UNESCO site, so it goes without saying that it's a must visit!

Name: Polonnaruwa Vatadage
User_Review:
The Polonnaruwa Vatadage is an ancient structure dating back to the Kingdom of Polonnaruwa of Sri Lanka. The meaning of Vatadage is 'The Circular Relic House'.
It is believed to have been built during the reign of Great King Parakramabahu to hold the Relic of the tooth of the Buddha or during the reign of Nissanka Malla of Polonnaruwa to hold the alms bowl used by the Buddha.

Name: Polonnaruwa Vatadage
User_Review: Polonnaruwa Vatadage is a well-preserved Vatadage in the famous Dalada Maluwa quadrangle of the ancient city of ruins, Polonnaruwa. Vatadages are typical Buddhist structures found in Sri Lanka. These are circular, constructed using stone and brick with intricate stone carvings and a wooden roof. They were built to protect stupas. Currently, there are ten Vatadages in Sri Lanka, and the most popular one is Polonnaruwa Vatadage.

Name: Polonnaruwa Vatadage
User_Review: The Polonnaruwa Vatadage is one of the most iconic and beautifully preserved structures in the ancient city. This circular relic house, believed to have once protected the Sacred Tooth Relic, showcases incredible stone carvings, guardstones, and moonstones. Four seated Buddha statues face each direction, symbolizing protection and enlightenment. The craftsmanship and spiritual atmosphere make it a must-visit highlight in Polonnaruwa. Ideal for history lovers, photographers, and anyone interested in ancient Buddhist architecture.

Name: Polonnaruwa Vatadage
User_Review: This historical attraction is worth a visit. Amazing early architecture. Cost was $30US to enter, with the ticket office at the museum. It's a large site so bike hire or tuk tuk is recommended. We got bikes outside for 1000 but they started at 1500rp. There are numerous stalls selling drinks , as well as pesky salesman trying to sell souvenirs. Toilets were clean and located in several areas. If you like history and Ruins this place is for you. The info sheet and signs don't really provide the best info so if you read up before you go you may better appreciate it, or you can hire a guide.

Name: Parakrama Samudra
User_Review:
"Peaceful and Historic Place"
Parakrama Samudraya is such a calm and beautiful spot to visit. The huge reservoir surrounded by nature gives a relaxing feeling, and the view is especially nice in the evening. It’s also an important historical creation, showing the greatness of ancient Sri Lankan engineering. Sometimes a bit busy with locals and travelers, but it doesn’t disturb the peaceful atmosphere. A must-see if you’re visiting Polonnaruwa.

Name: Parakrama Samudra
User_Review:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Parakrama Samudra is a magnificent reservoir in Polonnaruwa, offering stunning views and a serene atmosphere. Watching the sunset here is an absolute must—the sky reflecting on the water creates a truly breathtaking scene. The area is peaceful and perfect for relaxation, photography, or simply enjoying nature. A visit to this historic and scenic spot is highly recommended for anyone exploring Polonnaruwa.

Name: Parakrama Samudra
User_Review: The tank has great view in evenings. You can see my images and nice atmosphere at evening.Road is very narrow.

Name: Parakrama Samudra
User_Review:
Parakrama Samudraya, located in the ancient city of Polonnaruwa, is a magnificent man-made reservoir built during the reign of King Parakramabahu the Great. Often called the “Sea of King Parakrama,” it’s a true testament to the advanced engineering and visionary leadership of ancient Sri Lanka.

The vastness of the reservoir is awe-inspiring. As you stand near its edge, you feel a sense of peace and admiration for the harmony between nature and ancient civilization. The view at sunset is especially breathtaking, with the sky reflecting on the still waters.

This historic tank not only served as a vital source of irrigation but also symbolizes the wisdom of the king, who famously declared, “Not even a drop of rainwater should be allowed to flow into the sea without being used for the benefit of the people.”

It’s a must-visit for anyone exploring Polonnaruwa—perfect for photography, quiet moments, or learning about Sri Lanka’s hydraulic heritage.

Name: Parakrama Samudra
User_Review: Best place in Polonnaruwa 💕 Beautiful view .. You should come here and Feel this fresh air

Name: Minneriya National Park
User_Review: We had an amazing time with Hasi! Coming in the morning is a great idea as there are nearly no other people and it’s quieter. He knew all the spots and we saw so much wildlife. Also was great at explaining the elephants behavior and understanding these incredible animals. Would recommend to absolutely everyone this experience with him

Name: Minneriya National Park
User_Review:
After doing quite a bit of research on elephant safaris, we found Hasi with Aliya Tours—what a gem of a find. Based on glowing reviews, we reached out, and from the very first message, Hasi was responsive, thoughtful, and full of great suggestions. He recommended Hurulu Eco Park over some of the more crowded reserves, and he was spot on—we saw elephants in abundance, in their natural habitat, and it was genuinely magical.

What really set Hasi apart was how ethical and intentional he was throughout the experience. While other jeeps clustered together chasing the same sightings, Hasi took a different approach—respectful, calm, and strategic. He clearly prioritises the wellbeing of the animals, and it shows. His deep knowledge of the elephants and the surrounding wildlife was impressive, but it was his instinctive ability to navigate the park—finding wildlife before the crowds—that made this safari feel so personal and extraordinary.

There’s also a quiet humility about Hasi that made the whole experience even more special. We offered him a tip at the end of the trip, and not only did he hand us back our change, but he only accepted after we gently insisted.

A final thoughtful touch—on the drive back, he surprised us with fresh coconuts from a roadside stall, a simple yet memorable gesture that wrapped up the morning beautifully.

Incredible guide, incredible ethics, incredible experience. 10/10.

Name: Minneriya National Park
User_Review:
I had the best day !!!! I put 5 starts because I can’t put more but he deserves more ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️
We were the first ones arrived and we had the elephants for only us for more than an hour, we were really close to the elephants but always safe and without disturbing them in any way!! 🐘
The guide, Hasi, also showed me monkeys, crocodile, different type of birds, chameleon…
Hasi is really friendly and gives a lot of informations about elephants (and also birds etc). HIGHLY RECOMMENDED !!!
Thank you Hasi 🐘 🐘 🐘

Name: Minneriya National Park
User_Review:
We were lucky to have Hasi as our safari guide. While most people head to Minneriya, he suggested Kaudulla National Park — and we’re so glad we listened.

Minneriya had 400–500 jeeps crowding the area, but Kaudulla had just a few, making it peaceful and better for the elephants. We saw 60–70 elephants in their natural space, undisturbed.

Hasi genuinely cares about wildlife and your experience. One of the best guides we’ve had — highly recommend!

Name: Minneriya National Park
User_Review: Our elephant safari in Minneriya National Park with Hasi was a truly remarkable experience. He arrived promptly at our hotel, and the journey to the park entrance was swift and comfortable. Hasi proved to be an incredibly professional and safe driver, expertly navigating the park while also pointing out other wildlife along the way. His extensive knowledge of Minneriya was evident as he skilfully located several groups of elephants, ensuring we had ample opportunities for observation. We particularly appreciated Hasi's ethical approach; he approached the elephants slowly and carefully, prioritising their well-being and minimising any disturbance. This thoughtful and respectful interaction made our safari both enjoyable and responsible. We wholeheartedly recommend Hasi and Minneriya Elephant Safari for anyone seeking an unforgettable and ethical wildlife encounter. Be sure to book in advance, he is very responsive via Whatsapp.

Name: Puttalam Lagoon

Name: Kalpitiya Beach

Name: Wilpattu National Park Entry
User_Review:
I had an amazing 3-day stay exploring Wilpattu National Park, traveling over 235 km inside the park. The experience was unforgettable, with so many opportunities to watch wildlife in their natural surroundings.

One of the most special moments was spotting the famous tiger named Neluma—a truly breathtaking sight. Along the way, I also enjoyed seeing a wide variety of other animals and birds that make Wilpattu so unique.

The park is vast, peaceful, and filled with natural beauty, making it the perfect destination for anyone who loves wildlife and nature. Spending time here feels like stepping into another world, where every drive brings new surprises.

Name: Wilpattu National Park Entry
User_Review: Beautiful and wild national park! Amazing chance to see leopards, elephants, sloth bears, and many birds. Peaceful lakes and untouched jungle make it a must-visit for nature lovers

Name: Wilpattu National Park Entry
User_Review:
Wilpattu National Park , Peaceful and Scenic, But No Leopard Sightings
We visited Wilpattu National Park with high hopes of spotting leopards, especially since we entered early in the morning. Unfortunately, we didn’t see any during our trip. Sightings can be unpredictable and often depend heavily on the guide’s experience and tracking skills.
That said, the park itself is beautiful and tranquil. We were lucky to see elephants, crocodiles, monkeys, deer, and a wide variety of birds. The natural setting is peaceful and less crowded than other parks, which made the experience feel more authentic.
While we were disappointed not to see leopards, we still appreciated the wildlife we did encounter and the serene atmosphere of the park.

Name: Wilpattu National Park Entry
User_Review:
Fantastic park. Saw one leopard, many water buffalos, many peacocks and birds. What a drive through the “enchanted Forrest”

I found the smell of trees and vegetation amazing. The lakes here and there were simply superb.

Name: Wilpattu National Park Entry
User_Review:
Wilpattu is absolutely stunning! The park is peaceful, rich with wildlife, and beautifully maintained. We were lucky to see a bear, 3 leopards and many birds during our safari. Our guide was knowledgeable and respectful of the environment. A perfect destination for nature lovers and anyone looking to experience Sri Lanka’s wilderness at its best.

Highly recommend taking the evening safari. For local visitors, a half-day safari jeep costs around LKR 12,000, not including the park entrance fee.

Name: Munneswaram Kovil
User_Review: The Munneswaram Kovil, located in Chilaw, Sri Lanka, is a prominent religious site venerated by both Hindu and Buddhist devotees. It is dedicated to one of the five manifestations of Lord Shiva and stands as a symbol of interfaith harmony and cultural continuity. The temple’s intricate architecture and tranquil surroundings reflect Sri Lanka’s rich cultural and spiritual heritage. A field visit to this sacred site was conducted on the 5th of September, 2025.

Name: Munneswaram Kovil
User_Review:
Amazing Munneswaram temple in Sri Lanka. One of the five Shiva sthalas of Lanka. Ravana's mother worshipped this 12,000 year old Linga.
Blessed

Name: Munneswaram Kovil
User_Review:
I visited Munneswaram Kovil recently. I took a bus from Colombo to Chilaw, then another bus from Chilaw towards Kurunegala and asked the conductor to drop me near Munneswaram temple – very easy and affordable journey.

This is also one of the five Eeswaram temples in Sri Lanka, which makes it very special for devotees. Both Hindus and Buddhists visit here, especially during the big festivals like Navarathri, Sivarathri, and the famous Munneswaram festival.

The architecture and atmosphere are very beautiful, and I could feel the strong cultural and spiritual heritage. A must-visit if you are interested in history, religion, or Sri Lankan traditions.

Name: Munneswaram Kovil
User_Review:
Munneshwaram is a very attractive and sacred kovil I visited, and it truly feels special. Within the main premises, you will find many kovils, and even along the roadside outside, there are additional kovils to explore. This makes the whole area spiritually rich and culturally vibrant. Even if you are a Buddhist, you can also visit the temple with respect, worship, and experience the devotion here.

Before entering the kovil, you must clean your feet at the foot-washing area inside the premises, which is part of the tradition and respect. You can also make offerings to the gods and pray for your wishes to come true.

Parking facilities are available, and if you need fruits or other items for offerings, they can be easily purchased from the stalls near the vehicle park.

I highly recommend visiting Munneshwaram Kovil, not only to worship but also to experience the beauty of Hindu traditions, devotion, and the peaceful atmosphere that surrounds this sacred place. 🙏✨

Name: Munneswaram Kovil
User_Review:
🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟
Munneshwaram Kovil, Halawatha, Sri Lanka

I recently had the privilege of visiting Munneshwaram Kovil in Halawatha, and it was truly an inspiring experience. The shrine is beautifully maintained, with vibrant deities and serene surroundings that radiate spiritual energy. The atmosphere is peaceful and respectful, perfect for quiet contemplation or offering prayers.

The temple staff were courteous and helpful, guiding me through customs with gentle patience, and the ritual offerings (poonal) were well-organized. The temple grounds are tidy and well-cared-for, with lovely greenery and incense filling the air and adding to the sacred feel.

Parking was easy to locate nearby, and the temple’s location in Halawatha provides a calm, welcoming environment away from city noise. Whether you’re interested in cultural heritage, spirituality, or simply finding a moment of peace, Munneshwaram Kovil is a meaningful spot well worth a visit. 🙏

They were always cheerful and willing to help. The facilities at the hotel were well maintained and always clean. Our room was very comfortable. The hotel was definitely value for money, allowing us to stay for close to a month. The location for us was good, as it allowed us to make trips and explore local areas of interest. We will visit this hotel for certain in the future! Thanks very much to the manager Neil, Nimna, Rasika, Rukmal and Ajith for looking after us so well. Our bags and belongings were perfectly secure in our room, when we were not there. We fully recommend this hotel!

Name: Kurunegala Elephant Rock
User_Review: Quite a nice drive up. The folks selling their ware at the top don't bother you once you tell them you aren't buying anything, unlike at other places. Great view of the town.

Name: Kurunegala Elephant Rock
User_Review:
Climbing Athugala Rock in Kurunegala was an unforgettable experience! The "Elephant Rock" truly lives up to its name, a majestic natural wonder dominating the skyline. We opted for the stairs, and while it was a good workout, the gradual ascent through lush greenery was incredibly rewarding. Along the way, we spotted playful monkeys, adding to the natural charm.
Reaching the summit, the panoramic views of Kurunegala city and the surrounding landscape were simply breathtaking. The massive Samadhi Buddha statue standing proudly at the peak exudes a profound sense of peace. We timed our climb for sunset, and the sky transformed into a canvas of oranges and purples, casting a magical glow over the entire vista. It felt like the city lights twinkling below were welcoming us.
The descent, though steep in parts, was manageable with the handrails. While the power lines near the Buddha statue are a slight eyesore, they don't detract significantly from the overall awe-inspiring experience. Athugala isn't just a climb; it's a blend of spiritual tranquility, natural beauty, and a tangible connection to Sri Lanka's rich heritage. A definite must-do for anyone visiting Kurunegala!

Name: Kurunegala Elephant Rock
User_Review: This is the Land mark for Kurunegala city . Nice 360 view point can visit by vehicle or as well as a  hike through steps. Can see the over looking the entire city and the beautiful lake. Best time to visit there in the morning and late evening as it’s a rock, sun rice hard and feel very hot. Must visit the place when you go Kurunegala.

Name: Kurunegala Elephant Rock

Name: Yapahuwa Rock Fortress
User_Review:
I recently visited the Yapahuwa Rock Fortress, and it was truly an unforgettable experience. It's a historical fortress and a military stronghold built in 1272 by the King Buwanekabahu to protect from    foreign invaders, astonishing stone stair case with a steep slop make the fortress unique from others. If you're someone who enjoys history, nature, and a bit of adventure, this site has all three. Be prepared, though — there’s quite a bit of climbing involved!  The stone steps leading up the rock are steep and long, so comfortable shoes and plenty of water are highly recommended. Water is much needed.

About halfway up, the climb becomes somewhat challenging, especially with the wind blowing across the face of the rock. However, the effort is absolutely worth it. Once you reach the top, you’re rewarded with breathtaking panoramic views of the entire Yapahuwa area. You can even see the Daduru Oya reservoir from up there, which adds to the scenic beauty.

At the summit, there’s a small ancient pond and the ruins of an old brick stupa that lately built by the period of Kandian kingdom. The sense of calm and quiet at the top is a stark contrast to the effort it takes to get there.

Yapahuwa Rock Fortress is a hidden gem in Sri Lanka — full of history, natural beauty, and adventure. Definitely worth the climb!

Name: Yapahuwa Rock Fortress
User_Review: Yapahuwa is a breathtaking ancient rock fortress with deep historical roots. Climbing to the top rewards you with stunning views and a peaceful atmosphere. The stone staircase and preserved ruins showcase the artistic brilliance of our ancestors. A must-visit for anyone who loves culture, heritage, and adventure!

Name: Yapahuwa Rock Fortress
User_Review: A  bit of hiking with history! The climb involves number of steps leading to the top. there you can see ancient ruins and scenic views. It gets very hot during the day🥵🥵 so try to go early morning or late afternoon.

Name: Yapahuwa Rock Fortress
User_Review:
Must visit place if your a person interested in learning about Sri Lankan history & culture.
It’s a short climb but has very steep steps. Best to wear walking shoes.
It’s a place for everyone. Only very few people know and visit this historical location. Also there aren’t many places/ hotels to lodge if you’re visiting this place.

Name: Yapahuwa Rock Fortress
User_Review:
Yapahuwa Rock Fortress is one of Sri Lanka’s lesser-known but most fascinating historical sites. Located between Kurunegala and Anuradhapura, this ancient citadel was briefly the capital of Sri Lanka in the 13th century. Though it is often overlooked compared to Sigiriya, I found Yapahuwa to be equally breathtaking, with its dramatic rock setting, unique architecture, and quiet, off-the-beaten-path charm.

The fortress is built around a massive 90-meter-high rock, which rises suddenly from the surrounding plains. The climb begins at the impressive **stone stairway**, one of the most beautiful features of Yapahuwa. The staircase is adorned with intricate carvings of lions, dancers, and floral motifs, showcasing the incredible artistry of medieval Sri Lankan stonework. Climbing up, you can imagine how this fortress once served as a royal palace and stronghold to protect the Sacred Tooth Relic of the Buddha.

At the top of the rock, you’ll find the remains of the royal palace complex and enjoy sweeping panoramic views of the countryside. The landscape of lakes, paddy fields, and distant hills makes the climb well worth the effort. Unlike Sigiriya, Yapahuwa is less crowded, which allows you to explore peacefully and take in the atmosphere without distraction.

The site also has a small museum near the entrance, where you can see relics, sculptures, and artifacts uncovered from the fortress. These displays give valuable insight into the life and culture of the kingdom during its short but important period of glory.

What makes Yapahuwa so special is its combination of history, culture, and natural beauty. It feels like a hidden gem—majestic yet tranquil, ancient yet alive with stories.

Practical tips: Wear comfortable shoes for the climb, bring water, and try visiting in the morning or late afternoon to avoid the heat. The climb is moderately challenging, but the views and history make it truly rewarding.

For anyone traveling in Sri Lanka, Yapahuwa Rock Fortress is a must-see if you want to experience a unique piece of history away from the usual tourist trail.

Name: Ridi Viharaya Temple
User_Review: A calm and peaceful temple rich with ancient heritage. It's usually not crowded on regular days, except during Poya holidays. There’s ample parking available and a spacious rest area where visitors can stay overnight.

Name: Ridi Viharaya Temple
User_Review:
Ridi Vihareya nor” Waraka waladu Vihareya” is related to folk stories and has a historical value since 2 BC of king Dutugamunu Era. Temple has artistic value of ancient arts, crafts and ancient Buddha statues. An ancient temple is surrounded with breathtaking natural environment and we experienced peaceful religious surrounding.
If you interested in history and culture of Sri Lanka you must explore this ancient temple.

Name: Ridi Viharaya Temple
User_Review: This is a historical temple. You will find historical Buddhist statues and old buildings here. You can also see ancient Buddhist art on the rock cave walls, created by our ancestors using natural colors. The temple is located on a mountain, offering a beautiful view. Food and drinks are available at the parking area. It can get crowded on Poya days, but there is plenty of parking available at the entrance.

Name: Ridi Viharaya Temple
User_Review: Wonderful temple to visit!  They have very spacious grounds perfect for some walking meditation.  Also worth mentioning they offer free accommodation for those wishing to immerse themselves in the nature and temple surroundings!

History
This place an anciatet cave temple build in Anuradhapura Kingdom period and repaired during Kandy Kingdom period. In the folklore, This was the place where discovered silver to build up Ruwanwali sthupa ( pagoda).

Very good parking area and toilets are availble

Name: Nallur Kandaswamy Kovil
User_Review:
Nallur Kandhasamy Kovil, dedicated to Lord Murugan, is one of the most revered Hindu temples in Sri Lanka. It was originally founded in 948 AD by Bhuvanekabahu, then re-established in 1734 by Krishnaiyar, a minister to the Tamil king of Jaffna.

The temple was destroyed multiple times during colonial invasions — first by the Portuguese in the 16th century, then during various conquests. The current structure stands as a result of the 18th-century rebuilding, preserving the Dravidian architecture, towering gopuram, and intricate traditions.

Nallur is not only a religious center but also a cultural cornerstone for Tamil Hindus. Its grand Ther Thiruvizha (Chariot Festival) draws thousands annually, keeping the legacy alive.

Today, it remains a symbol of devotion, resilience, and Tamil heritage.

Name: Casuarina Beach
User_Review:
Casuarina Beach in Karainagar, Jaffna, remains one of the most peaceful and scenic beaches in Northern Sri Lanka. Known for its shallow, calm waters and long stretch of soft white sand, it’s a perfect getaway for families, couples, and nature lovers.

The beach is generally clean, with a relaxing atmosphere and fewer crowds compared to southern beaches. The tall casuarina trees lining the coast give great shade and a unique natural vibe.

In recent visits, basic facilities like parking and seating areas have slightly improved, but food stalls and washrooms are still minimal—so it’s best to bring your own water and snacks. The road to the beach is motorable but narrow in some places.

Best time to visit is early morning or late afternoon to avoid the heat. The sunset views are beautiful, and the beach is safe for wading, especially for children.

If you’re in Jaffna, Casuarina Beach is a must-visit for peace, beauty, and a calm seaside experience.

Name: Dambakola Patuna Temple
User_Review:
Jambukolapattinam Sangamiththa Buddhist Temple (also known as Dambakolapatuna Sangamitta Temple) is a revered Buddhist site located on the northern coast of Sri Lanka, near Kankesanthurai in the Jaffna District.

Overview:
This temple marks the historic landing place of Arahant Sangamitta Theri, the daughter of Emperor Ashoka of India, who arrived in Sri Lanka around the 3rd century BCE. She brought with her the sacred sapling of the Sri Maha Bodhi Tree from Bodh Gaya, which was later planted in Anuradhapura.

Significance:
It commemorates the arrival of Buddhism’s sacred Bodhi tree to Sri Lanka.
The temple stands as a symbol of Buddhist heritage and the deep cultural ties between ancient India and Sri Lanka.
Pilgrims visit here to pay homage to Sangamitta Theri and the sacred mission she undertook.

Location:
Region: Northern Province, Sri Lanka
Nearest town: Kankesanthurai (KKS)
Distance: About 20 km from Jaffna city

Present Temple:
The temple complex includes a replica of the sacred Bo tree, statues of Sangamitta Theri, and modern stupas and shrines overlooking the sea.
It is a peaceful and spiritually uplifting site, often visited by devotees and tourists alike.

Name: Dambakola Patuna Temple
User_Review: Jambukōlapattinam Sangamiththa Buddhist Temple is a sacred and historically significant place in Jaffna. It is believed to be where Sangamiththa Theri first arrived in Sri Lanka, bringing a sapling of the sacred Sri Maha Bodhi. The temple carries deep cultural and spiritual value, with a calm and peaceful atmosphere that makes it ideal for worship and reflection. Surrounded by serenity and well-preserved heritage, it is a must-visit for anyone interested in Buddhist history and spirituality. 🙏🌿✨

Name: Dambakola Patuna Temple
User_Review:
Sacred Shores of History and Peace – Dambakola Patuna

Dambakola Patuna, also known as Jambukola Patuna, is a deeply spiritual and historically significant site located on the northern coast of Sri Lanka, not far from Jaffna. It’s most famously known as the port where Theri Sanghamitta, daughter of Indian Emperor Ashoka, landed over 2,000 years ago, bringing with her a sapling of the sacred Bodhi tree — the very same species under which Lord Buddha attained enlightenment.
This event marked a monumental turning point in the history of Buddhism in Sri Lanka, and Dambakola Patuna is honored as a sacred gateway through which that spiritual legacy entered the island.

🌊 What You'll See:

A peaceful Buddhist temple (Dambakola Patuna Sanghamitta Temple) near the coastline, with a serene beachfront backdrop and swaying palm trees
A Bodhi tree believed to be connected to the original sapling brought by Sanghamitta Theri
A statue commemorating her arrival, and plaques explaining the historical and religious significance
Clean temple grounds and a quiet atmosphere, perfect for meditation or reflection

✨ Why It’s Worth Visiting:

It’s not just a religious site, but a place of national heritage and cultural pride
The surrounding scenery is beautiful and calming, with the ocean nearby adding to the spiritual atmosphere
It’s less crowded than many other religious sites, which makes it feel intimate and undisturbed

🧭 Tips for Travelers:

Located around 30–40 minutes from Jaffna town, the site is accessible by road — best to go by tuk-tuk or with a local driver
The area is clean and well-kept, but basic facilities only (no major shops or cafés nearby)
As with all Buddhist temples, dress modestly, remove shoes when entering sacred areas, and be respectful of those in prayer
Early morning or late afternoon visits are best, especially to avoid the heat
If you're visiting Jaffna and are interested in Buddhism, Sri Lankan history, or simply want to find a quiet, meaningful coastal spot, Dambakola Patuna is a must-see. It's not flashy or touristy — just peaceful, powerful, and rooted in centuries of spiritual heritage.

Name: Keerimalai Hot Springs
User_Review:
Sacred Springs with a Serene Atmosphere 💧

The Keerimalai Wells (also known as the Keerimalai Freshwater Springs) are a peaceful and sacred natural site located just north of Jaffna, right next to the Naguleswaram Shiva Temple and the coastline. These springs are believed to have healing properties, and for centuries, locals have bathed here as part of religious and cultural traditions.

The water is crystal clear and naturally fed, and it’s fascinating that the springs are right by the ocean, yet remain fresh due to an underground source. The separation of men's and women’s bathing areas allows for privacy, and there’s a quiet, spiritual vibe to the entire place — especially if you go early in the morning or on a weekday.

🌿 What to Expect:

The area is modest and traditional — don't expect resort-style pools, but rather natural, open-air baths used primarily by locals
Many people visit for ritual cleansing or spiritual reasons, especially before entering the nearby temple
The surrounding area is scenic, with sea views and palm trees adding to the charm
It’s best to dress modestly and bring your own towel or change of clothes

⚠️ Note:

The site could benefit from better maintenance and cleanliness — it’s a bit basic in terms of facilities
Some areas may be slippery or aged, so walk with care
If you're interested in local traditions, natural healing sites, or quiet spiritual places, Keerimalai Springs are definitely worth a visit. It’s not a long stop, but a meaningful one — especially when combined with Naguleswaram Kovil next door.

Name: Adams Bridge Sand Banks
User_Review:
The old bridge of Mannar was initially the only link connecting the mainland and Mannar Island, a structure of a single lane causeway and bridge which was constructed in the 1930s. During the civil war in 1990, the bridge had been destroyed by an explosions caused by the rebel group of Liberation Tamil Tigers if Eelam in which parts of it remained.

Sri Lanka security force had rebuilt a temporary bridge so as to provide an entry point for people to enter the island but its structure was not strong enough to hold vehicles over 10 tonnes.

Therefore, a plan was executed in 2007 to construct a new bridge in which construction had begun but had to be ceased due to increased tensions of the civil war. Building of the bridge was re-started as soon as the war had ended and was completed extending its length 3.14km longer and was officially opened in March 2010.

This two lane bridge is a part of the A14 Medawachchiya-Talaimannar highway. Mannar was an area that was difficult to develop as a destination due to lack of connection with the mainland, but now it is changing due to the new bridge hence the living standards of the people have improved and Mannar has become a newly discovered tourist destination.
